title: Rotational Mechanics
learningObjectives: Apply the rotational analogues of Newton's Second Law (τ = Iα)
Calculate torque and moment of inertia for common shapes
Use the law of conservation of angular momentum
introText: Rotational mechanics is the rotational analogue of translational mechanics. Every linear quantity has a rotational counterpart: displacement→angle, velocity→angular velocity, force→torque, mass→moment of inertia.

steps.heading: Rotational Quantities
steps.body:
Linear → Rotational analogues:

• Displacement (s, m) → Angle (θ, rad)
• Velocity (v, m/s) → Angular velocity (ω, rad/s)
• Acceleration (a, m/s²) → Angular acceleration (α, rad/s²)
• Mass (m, kg) → Moment of inertia (I, kg·m²)
• Force (F, N) → Torque (τ, N·m)
• Momentum (p) → Angular momentum (L, kg·m²/s)

The connection between linear and rotational: v = rω, a_t = rα, s = rθ.
steps.deepDive: The rotational SUVAT equations are identical in form to the linear ones with θ, ω, and α replacing s, v, and a: ω = ω₀ + αt; θ = ω₀t + ½αt²; ω² = ω₀² + 2αθ; θ = ½(ω₀ + ω)t.

steps.heading: Torque and Moment of Inertia
steps.body:
**Torque: τ = F × d⊥ = Fr sin θ**

Torque is the rotational analogue of force. The lever arm d⊥ is the perpendicular distance from the pivot to the line of action of the force.

**Newton's Second Law for rotation: τ_net = Iα**

Moment of inertia for common shapes (axis through centre unless noted):
• Solid cylinder: I = ½MR²
• Hollow cylinder (ring): I = MR²
• Solid sphere: I = ⅖MR²
• Rod about its centre: I = (1/12)ML²
• Rod about its end: I = (1/3)ML²

steps.heading: Angular Momentum and Its Conservation
steps.body:
**Angular momentum: L = Iω**

**In a closed system (no external net torque), angular momentum is conserved:**
I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂

A figure skater spins faster when arms are pulled in because I decreases, so ω must increase to keep L constant.

Example: I₁ = 4.0 kg·m², ω₁ = 1.5 rad/s → I₂ = 1.6 kg·m²
ω₂ = I₁ω₁/I₂ = (4.0 × 1.5)/1.6 = 3.75 rad/s
steps.deepDive: The parallel axis theorem: I = I_cm + Md², where d is the distance from the centre-of-mass axis to the new axis. This lets you compute I for any parallel axis.

steps.questionText: A torque of 12 N·m is applied to a flywheel with moment of inertia I = 3 kg·m². What angular acceleration results?
A solid cylinder of mass 2 kg and radius 0.3 m rotates at 6 rad/s. What is its angular momentum?

questions.questionText: A figure skater with I = 5 kg·m² spins at 2 rad/s and pulls in arms to reduce I to 2 kg·m². What is the new angular velocity?
questions.explanation: Conservation of angular momentum: I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂ → ω₂ = (5 × 2)/2 = 5 rad/s.

questions.questionText: A rod of mass 3 kg and length 2 m rotates about its end. What is its moment of inertia?
